content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 1,
1992
Martin B. Miller, President
Willow Grove Union School Board
14865 Panoche Road
Paicines,
CA
95043
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—92—437
Dear Mr. Miller:
This letter is in response to your request for advice under
the conflict—of-interest pzOVisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
!
Since you are seeking general assistance and not
inquiring about a specific decision before the districtL your
letter is considered a request for informal assistance.’/
Q!JST ION
Is it a conflict of interest for a school board member to
vote on the budgetary process if
a school board member has
a
spouse employed by the district?
CONCLUS I ON
Salary from a local government agency,
including
a school
district,
is not
income under the Political
Reform Act.
FACTS
The Willow Grove Union School District has
a board member
whose wife is employed by the district.
Recently,
the San Benito
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California
Code of Regulations.
2/
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal :ritten advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812
•
(916) 322-5660


Advice i’iie iso. i--’i.
Page Two
County Grand Jury requested several districts to explore the
possibility that they are in violation of conflict of interest
rules.
-
You are asking if there is a potential for a conflict of
interest if a board member participates in decisions concerning
the district’s budget, if those decisions also affect the salary
of the board member’s spouse,
who is an employee of the district.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they know or have reason to know
they have a financial interest.
An official has
a financial interest in
a governmental
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the official,
or on a member of the official’s immediate
family,
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has
a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to tfie public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or
agent for
a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to,
received by,
or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.


Aavlce iie rio.
Page Three
The definition of “income” under the Act provides that
the income of
an individual also includes any community
.property interest in the income of a spouse.
Any person or
business who has been a source of income to an official’s
spouse of $500 or more within the past
12 months,
is
a
potentially disqualifying ecOnomic interest as described in
Section 87103(c).
Regulation 18702.1(c).
The Act expressly exempts salary from a state,
local or
federal government agency from the definition of “income” for
purposes of the Act.
(Section 82030(b)(2).)
Thus,
the
spouse’s salary from the school district would not create
a
conflict of interest.
However,
the board member may not
participate in decisions to hire, fire,
promote,
demote,
or
discipline his or her spouse,
or to set a salary for the
spouse which is different from salaries paid to other
employees of the district in the same job classification or
position.
This advice is limited to the provisions of the Act.
Other legal provisions,
such as Government Code Section 1090,
may restrict the board member’s ability to participate in the
decision.
The Commission,
however,
does not administer this
section.
The district attorney and the Attorney General are
responsible for enforcing Section 1090.
I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance
you requested.
If you have further questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
ci LLC7LLCL
3 anette
E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
JET! jt

sections.conclusion:
Salary from a local government agency,
including
a school
district,
is not
income under the Political
Reform Act.

embedding.qa_text:
CONCLUSION: Salary from a local government agency,
including
a school
district,
is not
income under the Political
Reform Act.
